section.title: Saul Anointed King
section.sectionRationale: Samuel privately anoints Saul with oil and gives him signs to confirm God's call; the Spirit rushes on Saul and changes him. Then, publicly at Mizpah, Samuel again names Israel's demand as a rejection of God, yet casts lots, and Saul, hiding among the baggage, is chosen and proclaimed king. Some rejoice, some despise him. It is one movement: Saul is set apart as king, privately by anointing and Spirit, publicly by lot and acclamation, under the shadow of Israel's rejection of God.
section.thesis: God confirms His chosen ruler by anointing, sign, and Spirit, and openly identifies him before all Israel, yet Samuel keeps insisting that the day is one of rejection, so that Saul's coronation is joy and warning together, a king given by grace to a people who have spurned their true King.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the anointing and the promised signs, verses 1 to 8; the Spirit transforms Saul, verses 9 to 16; Saul chosen by lot at Mizpah, verses 17 to 24; and the rights of kingship and mixed response, verses 25 to 27.

units.label: The anointing and the promised signs
units.note: the LORD anointed you ruler over His inheritance

verses.text: Then Samuel took a flask of oil, poured it on Saul’s head, kissed him, and said, “Has not the LORD {{mashach|anointed}} you ruler over His inheritance?

When you leave me today, you will find two men at Rachel’s tomb in Zelzah on the border of Benjamin. They will say to you, ‘The donkeys you seek have been found, and now your father has stopped worrying about the donkeys and started worrying about you, asking, “What should I do about my son?”’

Then you will go on from there until you come to the Oak of Tabor. Three men going up to God at Bethel will meet you there, one carrying three young goats, another carrying three loaves of bread, and another carrying a skin of wine.
They will greet you and give you two loaves of bread, which you will accept from their hands.

After that you will come to Gibeah of God, where the Philistines have an outpost. As you approach the city, you will meet a group of prophets coming down from the high place, preceded by harps, tambourines, flutes, and lyres, and they will be prophesying.
Then the {{ruach|Spirit of the LORD}} will rush upon you, and you will prophesy with them; and you will be transformed into a different person.
When these signs have come, do as the occasion demands, for God is with you.
And you shall go before me to Gilgal, and surely I will come to you to offer burnt offerings and to sacrifice peace offerings. Wait seven days until I come to you and show you what you are to do.”
As Saul turned to leave Samuel, God changed Saul’s heart, and all the signs came to pass that day.
When Saul and his servant arrived at Gibeah, a group of prophets met him. Then the Spirit of God rushed upon him, and he prophesied along with them.
When all those who had formerly known Saul saw him prophesying with the prophets, they asked one another, “What has happened to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets?”
Then a man who lived there replied, “And who is their father?” So the saying became a proverb: “Is Saul also among the prophets?”
And when Saul had finished prophesying, he went up to the high place.
Now Saul’s uncle asked him and his servant, “Where did you go?” “To look for the donkeys,” Saul replied. “When we saw they were not to be found, we went to Samuel.”
“Tell me,” Saul’s uncle asked, “what did Samuel say to you?”
And Saul replied, “He assured us that the donkeys had been found.” But Saul did not tell his uncle what Samuel had said about the kingship.

units.label: Saul chosen by lot at Mizpah
units.note: Do you see the one the LORD has chosen

verses.text: After this, Samuel summoned the people to the LORD at Mizpah

and said to the Israelites, “This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: ‘I brought Israel up out of Egypt, and I rescued you from the hands of the Egyptians and of all the kingdoms that oppressed you.’

But today you have rejected your God, who saves you from all your troubles and afflictions, and you have said to Him, ‘No, set a king over us.’ Now therefore present yourselves before the LORD by your tribes and clans.”
Thus Samuel had all the tribes of Israel come forward, and the tribe of Benjamin was selected.
Then he had the tribe of Benjamin come forward by its clans, and the clan of Matri was selected. Finally, Saul son of Kish was selected. But when they looked for him, they could not find him.
So again they inquired of the LORD, “Has the man come here yet?” And the LORD replied, “Behold, he has hidden himself among the baggage.”
So they ran and brought Saul, and when he stood among the people, he was a head taller than any of the others.
Samuel said to all the people, “Do you see the one the LORD has {{bachar|chosen}}? There is no one like him among all the people.” And all the people shouted, “Long live the king!”

units.label: The rights of kingship and mixed response
units.note: How can this man save us

verses.text: Then Samuel explained to the people the rights of kingship. He wrote them on a scroll and laid it up before the LORD. And Samuel sent all the people away, each to his own home.
Saul also went to his home in Gibeah, and the men of valor whose hearts God had touched went with him.
But some worthless men said, “How can this man save us?” So they despised him and brought him no gifts; but Saul remained silent about it.

mashach.gloss: To anoint, smear with oil. The pouring of oil on Saul's head sets him apart as the LORD's chosen ruler over His inheritance, God's own people. Anointing marks kings and priests as belonging to God and equipped by His Spirit; the anointed one, mashiach, ultimately points to Christ, the true and lasting Anointed King.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The Spirit of the LORD rushes upon Saul, empowering him for his office and changing him into a different man. In the Old Testament the Spirit came upon judges and kings to enable God's work; here the same Spirit confirms Saul's call, though the Spirit given to Saul can later depart, unlike the abiding Spirit poured out in Christ.

bachar.gloss: To choose, select. Samuel presents Saul as the one the LORD has chosen; the king is God's choice, not merely the people's. Yet the same book will show that God's deepest choice falls on David, a man after His own heart, revealing that God chooses by the heart and directs His choosing toward the true King, Christ.

basic.context: Samuel anoints Saul in private, pouring oil on his head and naming him the LORD's chosen ruler over God's people. He gives Saul three signs to confirm the call, including that the Spirit of God will rush on him and change him into a different man, which happens that very day. Then Samuel gathers all Israel at Mizpah. He again tells them plainly that in demanding a king they have rejected the God who saves them, yet he proceeds to identify the king by lot. The lot falls on Saul, who is found hiding among the baggage. Tall and striking, he is proclaimed king with shouts of Long live the king, though some scoff, how can this man save us? The question is: how does God set apart the king Israel demanded? By His own anointing, Spirit, and choice, even while warning that the day is one of rejection.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that holds two things together: God genuinely gives and equips Saul, and yet the whole enterprise stands under the verdict that Israel has rejected God. We should not flatten either side. The Spirit rushing on Saul is real empowering for office, not the same as saving regeneration, since this Spirit can later depart. The signs, the lot, and the anointing all show God's hand. The mixed response at the end honestly previews the reign to come. Its enduring truths are that authority comes from God and that outward gifting is not the same as a faithful heart.
basic.originalLanguages: Samuel pours oil to mashach, anoint, Saul as the LORD's chosen ruler; the word marks him as set apart to God, and its noun form is messiah. The ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes on Saul to empower him and transform him for a day. And Samuel presents him as the one the LORD has bachar, chosen; the king is God's choice, though the book will show God's deepest choice resting on David and pointing to Christ.
basic.typology: Saul, anointed with oil and rushed upon by the Spirit for his office, is a shadow of the true Anointed One, on whom the Spirit rests without measure and without ever departing. The king proclaimed before all the people, yet doubted by some, how can this man save us, foreshadows Christ, God's chosen King, acclaimed by some and despised by others. And the failure that will follow Saul's gifted beginning drives the longing for the King whose heart matches His anointing.
basic.christ: Saul is anointed and Spirit-empowered, yet his gifting is not a new heart, and the Spirit given to him can be lost. Christ is the true Anointed, on whom the Spirit descended and remained, and who could not fail. Saul is presented as the LORD's chosen, but the choosing narrows through David to Jesus, God's beloved and truly chosen One. When scoffers ask, how can this man save us, they unknowingly voice the question the gospel answers: it is not Saul but Christ who can and does save. The oil on Saul's head points to the Messiah on whom it all depends.
basic.god: God is the one who appoints and equips rulers, confirming His choice by signs, Spirit, and lot, so that no one can doubt His hand. He is sovereign even over the casting of lots, directing them to His chosen man. He is honest, letting Samuel name the day's rejection even in the moment of coronation. And He touches the hearts of some to follow the king, showing that loyalty itself is His gift.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 16 records the Spirit rushing on David and later departing from Saul. Isaiah 11 promises the Spirit resting on the Messiah. Proverbs 16 says the lot's decision is from the LORD. And John 1 shows the Spirit descending and remaining on Jesus.
basic.oneStory: God sets apart Saul as king by anointing, Spirit, and lot, giving Israel the ruler they demanded even under the verdict of rejection. Yet the anointing oil, the empowering Spirit, and the language of God's chosen all reach beyond Saul to David and, at last, to Christ, the true Anointed King on whom the Spirit rests forever and who alone can save.

context.literary: This chapter narrates Saul's anointing and public accession, continuing the account of his rise, chapters 9 to 11. It joins private confirmation, the anointing and signs, with public confirmation, the lot at Mizpah. Samuel's renewed charge of rejection, verses 18 to 19, ties back to chapter 8, and the mixed response, verse 27, sets up the vindication of Saul in chapter 11.
context.historical: Anointing with oil formally installed kings in Israel and the ancient Near East. Prophetic bands, accompanied by music and ecstatic prophecy, were known in this period. Casting lots was a recognized means of seeking God's decision. The rights of kingship written on a scroll and stored before the LORD reflect the law's provision that the king be bound under God's word, Deuteronomy 17. Gibeah was Saul's hometown.
context.canonical: The anointing and Spirit-empowerment of the king develop the messianic thread from Hannah's song and the oracle to Eli. The pattern of the Spirit coming upon a king, and the later warning that He can depart from Saul, prepares for the promise of a Spirit-anointed Messiah in Isaiah and its fulfillment in Jesus, on whom the Spirit remains.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Saul is genuinely set apart as king by God's anointing, Spirit, and choice, while keeping before the reader that the day of coronation is also a day of Israel's rejection of God, and to introduce the tension between outward gifting and a faithful heart.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Saul's anointing and the Spirit rushing upon him; it does not prescribe seeking ecstatic experiences or casting lots for guidance today. What endures is that authority is given by God, that His Spirit equips for service, and that spiritual gifting must not be mistaken for a transformed, faithful heart.

debates.question: What does it mean that the Spirit rushed on Saul and he was changed into a different man?

views.label: Empowerment for office, not saving regeneration
views.case: The Spirit came upon judges and kings to equip them for a task; Saul is enabled and outwardly transformed for kingship, but this is not the same as a new heart, since the same Spirit later departs from him, 1 Samuel 16:14.

views.label: A genuine, if temporary, work confirming his call
views.case: God really changed Saul's heart, verse 9, and the Spirit's coming authenticated him before the people; the change was real and God-given, even though it did not guarantee lifelong faithfulness.

debates.bearing: Both agree that spiritual empowerment is not the same as persevering faith; Saul's gifting warns that being used by God is no substitute for a heart that follows Him, and points to the abiding Spirit given in Christ.

debates.question: Why does Samuel proclaim the king yet also declare the day a rejection of God?

views.label: To hold grace and warning together
views.case: God graciously gives and equips the king the people demanded, yet Samuel refuses to let them forget that the request rejected God's own kingship; the coronation is mercy and rebuke at once.

views.label: To bind the monarchy under God from the start
views.case: By naming the rejection and writing the rights of kingship before the LORD, Samuel sets the king under God's word and verdict, so the monarchy begins accountable to God rather than autonomous.

debates.bearing: Either way the chapter teaches that God's gifts do not cancel His truth; the king and the people stand under God's word, and true kingship must submit to the LORD, fulfilled in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the chapter. Samuel takes a flask of oil to mashach, to anoint, Saul, pouring it on his head and naming him ruler over the LORD's inheritance; anointing sets a man apart as God's own chosen and equipped one, and the noun from this verb is mashiach, messiah. Then the ruach, the Spirit of the LORD, rushes upon Saul, and he is transformed into a different man; in the Old Testament the ruach comes on judges and kings to empower them for God's work, though, as this same book will show, the Spirit given to Saul can later depart, unlike the Spirit who rests and remains on Christ. Finally Samuel presents Saul as the one the LORD has bachar, chosen; the king is God's choice, not merely the people's preference. Yet the reader who knows the story feels the tension already, for the same verb will describe God's deeper choice of David, the man after His own heart, and the choosing runs on to Jesus, God's beloved and truly Chosen One.

typology.type: The king anointed with oil and rushed upon by the Spirit
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Anointed One, on whom the Spirit descended and remained without measure, Isaiah 61:1 and John 1:32-33.

typology.type: The chosen king acclaimed by some and despised by others
typology.fulfillment: Christ, God's chosen King, received by some and rejected by others, 1 Peter 2:4-8.

typology.type: The gifted king whose heart will not match his calling
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the King whose heart perfectly matches His anointing and who cannot fail, Hebrews 1:8-9.

study.christ: Saul is anointed with oil and the Spirit of the LORD rushes upon him, yet his transformation is for office, not a new and lasting heart, and this Spirit can be lost, as chapter 16 will show. Christ is the true Anointed, the Messiah to whom the oil points, on whom the Spirit descended and remained without measure, John 1:32-33, and who never failed in the calling He received. Saul is presented as the one the LORD has chosen, but the divine choosing narrows through David, the man after God's heart, and comes to rest on Jesus, God's beloved and Chosen One, Luke 9:35. Even the scoffers' sneer, how can this man save us, unknowingly frames the gospel's answer: not Saul, and no merely gifted man, but Christ alone can and does save His people. The flask of oil emptied on Saul's head points beyond him to the King on whom everything depends.
study.god: This chapter shows God as the sovereign appointer and equipper of rulers. He confirms His chosen king by three fulfilled signs, by the rushing of His Spirit, and by the falling of the lot, so that no one can doubt His hand is behind the throne. He governs even the casting of lots, directing them unerringly to His man, and He knows exactly where the reluctant Saul is hiding. He is honest and holy, allowing His prophet to name the coronation day as a day of rejection, refusing to let His gifts silence His truth. And He is the God who touches hearts, moving men of valor to follow the king, so that even faithful loyalty is His gift. He gives, He equips, He warns, and He remains Lord over the monarchy He permits.

crossReferences.note: The Spirit rushes on David and departs from Saul, showing that Saul's empowering was real but not permanent.
The Spirit of the LORD rests on the coming Messiah, the abiding anointing that Saul's temporary one foreshadows.
The lot is cast, but its every decision is from the LORD, who directs it to His chosen king.
Christ, the chosen King, is precious to some and rejected by others, as Saul is acclaimed yet despised.

study.oneStory: God sets Saul apart as king by anointing, Spirit, lot, and public choice, granting Israel the ruler they demanded even under the verdict that they have rejected their true King. Yet the oil, the Spirit, and the language of God's chosen all strain beyond this tall, gifted, but flawed man toward David and, at last, toward Christ, the true Anointed King on whom the Spirit rests forever and who alone answers the scoffers' question by actually saving His people.

quiz.q: How did Samuel first set Saul apart as king?

options.text: He privately anointed Saul with oil as the LORD's chosen ruler and gave him confirming signs.
options.note: Verses 1 to 8; the anointing and signs mark Saul as God's chosen before the public event.

options.text: He poured oil on Saul's head and called him ruler over God's inheritance.
options.note: True, but omits the confirming signs, including the Spirit, that accompanied the anointing.

options.text: He crowned Saul publicly before all Israel first.
options.note: The public choosing came later at Mizpah; the anointing was first and private.

options.text: He had the people vote for Saul as king.
options.note: Saul was chosen by God through anointing and lot, not by a vote.

quiz.q: What happened when the Spirit of the LORD rushed upon Saul?

options.text: He prophesied and was transformed into a different man, empowered for his office.
options.note: Verses 6 and 9 to 10; the Spirit equipped and changed Saul for kingship.

options.text: He prophesied along with the group of prophets.
options.note: True, but omits the deeper point that he was transformed and empowered for his calling.

options.text: He was permanently made righteous and could never fail.
options.note: The empowering was real but not a guarantee of faithfulness; the Spirit later departs from Saul.

options.text: Nothing visible happened to him at all.
options.note: The change was evident enough that people asked, is Saul also among the prophets?

quiz.q: What did Samuel declare even as Saul was made king at Mizpah?

options.text: That in demanding a king the people had rejected the God who saves them.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 name the day's rejection even amid the coronation.

options.text: That God had brought Israel up out of Egypt and rescued them.
options.note: True, and Samuel said it, but the pointed word was that they had now rejected that God.

options.text: That the people had done well to ask for a king.
options.note: The opposite; Samuel called it a rejection of God.

options.text: That Saul would be a perfect and faithful king.
options.note: Samuel gave no such assurance; he warned of rejection and wrote the rights of kingship.

quiz.q: How did the people respond to Saul as king?

options.text: Many shouted Long live the king and followed him, but some scoffers said, how can this man save us?
options.note: Verses 24 to 27 show a divided response, joy and contempt together.

options.text: The whole nation joyfully accepted him without exception.
options.note: Most acclaimed him, but the text notes worthless men who despised him.

options.text: Everyone rejected Saul and refused to follow him.
options.note: The opposite; men of valor followed him and the people shouted Long live the king.

options.text: They immediately removed him from the throne.
options.note: Saul was proclaimed king, not removed.

quiz.q: How does Saul's anointing and empowering point forward to Christ?

options.text: The oil and the Spirit point to Christ, the true Anointed on whom the Spirit remains and who alone can save.
options.note: Saul's temporary anointing foreshadows the Messiah's abiding one, John 1:32-33.

options.text: It shows that God equips leaders with His Spirit for their work.
options.note: True, but stops short of Christ, the true Anointed the oil and Spirit foreshadow.

options.text: It teaches that anyone filled with the Spirit becomes sinless.
options.note: Saul's later failure shows empowering is not the same as a perfected heart.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The anointing and Spirit clearly point to the Messiah, Jesus.

questions: The Spirit changed Saul, yet his heart later turned away. How does his story warn you that being gifted or used by God is not the same as walking faithfully with Him?
Saul was proclaimed king even as Samuel called the day a rejection of God. Where might God be graciously giving you what you asked for, while calling you to examine your heart?
Some followed Saul because God touched their hearts, while others despised him. What softens or hardens a heart toward God's appointed King, Jesus?
Saul hid among the baggage on the day of his calling. When has fear or reluctance made you hide from what God was calling you to, and how did God draw you out?
The scoffers asked, how can this man save us? How does the gospel answer that question, not in Saul but in Christ, the King who truly saves?

translationNotes.note: The Hebrew is brief; the BSB footnote records a longer Septuagint reading that expands the anointing charge, but the main text follows the Hebrew, anointed you ruler over His inheritance.
Gibeah of God renders the Hebrew Gibeath-Elohim, the hill of God; the place names playing on the hill recur through the chapter.
They inquired of the LORD, likely by lot or oracle, and were told Saul had hidden among the baggage, underscoring both God's knowledge and Saul's reluctance.

A Dead Sea Scrolls manuscript adds a paragraph here about Nahash the Ammonite oppressing Gad and Reuben, giving background to the siege of Jabesh-gilead in chapter 11; the BSB follows the Masoretic Text and Septuagint, which omit it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 10:1-27. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
